title: Social Media Without the Stress: Content That Connects
subtitle: You do not need to be an influencer. You need to show people what it is like to visit your cafe.

toc.label: Why Social Media Matters for a Cafe
Content Ideas That Never Run Out

sections.heading: Why Social Media Matters for a Cafe
sections.body:
Social media is not about vanity metrics. For a cafe, it serves three practical functions:

**1. Discovery.** When someone searches "coffee shop" on Instagram or Google Maps, the cafes that appear with appealing photos and recent activity get the visit. A dormant profile with photos from 2022 does not inspire a detour.

**2. Expectation-setting.** Social media shows potential customers what to expect: the quality of the coffee, the look of the food, the vibe of the space. When reality matches the feed, trust builds. When the feed overpromises, reviews suffer.

**3. Relationship maintenance.** Your regulars follow you. They see your stories, your new menu items, your team. This keeps your cafe in their mind between visits. A story about fresh pastries at 8am reminds them to stop by.

You do not need to post daily. You do not need to go viral. You need a consistent presence that shows your cafe at its best, 3–5 times a week. That is achievable for any cafe without a marketing team.

sections.heading: Choosing Your Platform
sections.body:
You do not need to be on every platform. Choose one or two and do them well.

**Instagram:** The default for cafes. Visual, aesthetic, and where food and drink content thrives. Use for: beautiful photos of drinks and food, behind-the-scenes stories, team features, new menu announcements. Reels (short videos) get the most reach.

**Google Business Profile:** Not technically social media, but functionally it is your most important online presence. Every "near me" search goes through Google. Post updates weekly, respond to reviews, keep information current.

**TikTok:** If you have a team member who enjoys making short videos, TikTok can deliver enormous reach. Latte art videos, "day in the life" content, and ASMR coffee-making routines perform well. But it requires regular video content — if nobody on the team enjoys this, skip it.

**Facebook:** Declining for discovery but still useful for local community groups, events, and older demographics. A Facebook page with your hours, menu, and occasional posts is sufficient.

**What to skip:** Twitter/X (low relevance for cafes), LinkedIn (unless you do corporate catering), Pinterest (nice but not a customer driver).

**The 80/20 rule:** 80% of your social media value comes from Instagram and Google Business Profile. Focus there.

sections.heading: Photography That Sells
sections.body:
Use T138-01 (Cafe Photography Guide):

You do not need a professional camera. A modern smartphone with good lighting produces excellent cafe photography. What matters is technique.

**The rules:**

**Natural light.** Always. Move the drink to the window. Turn off overhead fluorescents. Natural light makes food and drinks look warm, rich, and appetising. Flash makes them look clinical.

**Clean background.** Move clutter out of the frame. A flat white on a clean marble counter looks beautiful. The same flat white next to a pile of receipts and a dirty cloth does not.

**Angles.** Three angles work for cafe content:
- 45 degrees (the way you naturally look at a drink on a table)
- Directly above (flat lay — great for food, pastry displays, table settings)
- Eye level (drinks in hand, counter shots, latte art)

**Styling.** A small plant, a napkin, a spoon, a book — simple props add context and warmth. Do not over-style. The goal is "natural moment" not "catalogue shoot."

**Edit lightly.** Increase brightness and warmth slightly. Add a touch of contrast. Do not over-filter — customers should recognise the food when it arrives.

**Batch shoot monthly.** Spend 30 minutes once a month photographing your best items in good light. This gives you a library of content to post throughout the month. Supplement with spontaneous phone shots during service.

**Video:** Short clips of latte art being poured, pastries coming out of the oven, or the cafe filling up on a Saturday morning. 15–30 seconds, no narration needed. The visual is enough.

sections.heading: Content Ideas That Never Run Out
sections.body:
The biggest obstacle to posting is not time — it is ideas. Use this rotation to never run out:

**Monday: Product spotlight.** A beautiful photo of a drink or food item. Name it, describe it briefly. "Our house flat white. 18g of our single-origin Colombian, microfoam, served in our favourite cup."

**Tuesday: Behind the scenes.** The team calibrating the grinder. The morning delivery. The display being built. Customers love seeing how the sausage is made (figuratively).

**Wednesday: Team feature.** A photo of a team member with a short quote. "This is Jordan. Favourite drink: oat cortado. Working here since January. Best shift: Saturday morning."

**Thursday: Customer moment.** A photo of a full cafe, a happy table, a dog in the window (with permission). This is social proof — potential customers see real people enjoying your space.

**Friday: Weekend invite.** What is on this weekend? New special? Event? Late opening? "Weekend plans? We have fresh cinnamon buns and a new guest coffee from [roaster]. See you Saturday."

**Seasonal content:** Seasonal drink launches, holiday decorations, weather-reactive posts ("Rain outside? Perfect flat white weather.").

**Stories (daily if possible):** Quick, informal, disposable. The morning setup, the first coffee of the day, the display cabinet looking full, a happy moment. Stories create a sense of live connection.

Not every post needs to be a masterpiece. Consistency matters more than perfection.

sections.heading: Posting Rhythm and Planning
sections.body:
Use T139-01 (Content Calendar Template):

Plan a week of content at a time. Sunday evening, 20 minutes:

1. Choose 3–5 posts from your content rotation
2. Select photos from your library or schedule shoots
3. Write captions (2–3 sentences maximum)
4. Schedule using a tool (Later, Buffer, Meta Business Suite) or set phone reminders

**Optimal posting times (varies — test yours):**
- Morning: 7:30–8:30am (people checking phones on commute)
- Lunchtime: 12–1pm (lunch break browsing)
- Evening: 6–8pm (relaxation scrolling)

**Engagement:**
- Respond to every comment (even a simple "Thanks!") within 24 hours
- Reply to DMs — these are often customers with questions or booking requests
- Like and comment on other local businesses' posts (builds community, increases visibility)
- Repost customer content that features your cafe (with credit)

**What to avoid:**
- Posting only when you have something to sell (feels like advertising)
- Going silent for weeks then posting 5 things in a day
- Negative or complaining posts
- Political or divisive content
- Photos that do not match the reality of your cafe

**Delegation:** A team member with a good eye and a personal social media presence is often better at this than the owner. Give them guidelines, review their first few posts, then trust them.

sections.heading: Google Business Profile Mastery
sections.body:
Use T134-01 (Google Business Audit):

Your Google Business Profile is more important than Instagram for driving new customers. It is the first thing people see in a "near me" search.

**Monthly audit:**
- Hours correct? (including holidays and seasonal changes)
- Phone number and website link working?
- Photos current? (upload 3–5 new photos monthly)
- Menu uploaded and current?
- All attributes set? (wifi, outdoor seating, dog-friendly, etc.)
- Recent posts published? (weekly Google posts boost visibility)

**Google Posts:** Treat these like mini-ads. Post weekly about:
- New menu items
- Events or special hours
- Seasonal offerings
- Behind-the-scenes moments
- Customer testimonials (with permission)

Each post should include a photo and a call to action ("Visit us this weekend", "Try our new seasonal latte").

**Q&A section:** Google allows anyone to ask and answer questions about your business. Monitor this — unanswered questions look bad, and incorrect answers from the public can mislead customers. Answer every question yourself.

**Insights:** Google provides data on how many people found your listing, what they searched for, and what actions they took (called, visited website, requested directions). Review monthly. If searches are declining, consider whether your profile needs refreshing or your SEO keywords need updating.

The cafe with 4.7 stars, 400 reviews, fresh photos, and weekly posts will always outperform the one with 3.9 stars, 20 reviews, and a photo from opening day. Google rewards activity, recency, and relevance.
